caveats: a: The occupation mix covers all warehousing and storage, not 493190 or the selected subsegment.
a: The fixed labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a cross-code IPS harmonization, so its downstream economic weight has a vintage and mapping mismatch.
a: The specialized-goods lens itself spans tank, yard, building, and high-security operating models with different task mixes.
rho: The evidence establishes capability and direction, not a representative adoption rate.
rho: Some productivity gains attributed to AI are inseparable from conventional WMS, sensors, robotics, and process redesign.
rho: Hazardous-material and oil-storage obligations can delay or constrain autonomous operation.
e: No public source provides the selected subsegment's share of current LMM firms.
e: The 246-firm frozen estimate uses a transportation-sector EBITDA-to-sales margin bridge that may misclassify asset-heavy specialty-storage operators.
e: NAICS assignment may follow an establishment's primary activity even when the parent combines storage, transport, wholesaling, or real estate.
s5: One adjacent transaction cannot establish an industry transfer rate.
s5: The cited target included transportation and temperature-controlled activities outside the selected lens.
s5: Private asset sales and undisclosed family transfers may be absent from public sources.
q: The source is a service provider's guide and may present favorable marketing claims.
q: Chemical, petroleum, automobile, lumber, and beverage-storage contracts may price and allocate savings differently.
q: The estimate concerns retained unit-economics benefit, not demand growth or throughput gains.
d5: BLS output projections cover all warehousing and storage and are not quantity forecasts for the selected lens.
d5: Nominal revenue, real output, capacity, throughput, and storage-days can diverge materially.
d5: The petroleum component faces transition and regulatory risks that may differ sharply by product and geography.
o: Operator-required service does not imply full-time labor at every site; one team may supervise multiple facilities remotely.
o: EPA SPCC applicability depends on capacity, location, and discharge risk and does not cover every facility in the lens.
o: Customer insourcing can remove outsourced quantity without eliminating the underlying physical-storage task.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Industrial, commercial, and institutional customers outsource custody and handling of specialized goods. Revenue generally combines recurring time- or capacity-based storage charges with receiving, movement, loadout, transaction, compliance, and other value-added activity fees; dedicated arrangements may use negotiated all-inclusive or cost-plus contracts.
lens.deviation_from_default: The Census code explicitly combines household-goods storage, paper-document storage, and specialized physical-goods storage. I narrow to specialized physical goods because records and household-goods businesses have materially different demand, labor, technology-substitution, asset, and contracting economics. The narrowing is for analytical coherence, not attractiveness, and uncertainty is widened because public data generally aggregate the full code or broader warehousing sector.
lens.excluded_activities: Paper-document and records storage; household-goods storage and moving; general merchandise, refrigerated, and farm-product warehousing; self-storage real estate; hazardous-waste treatment; merchant petroleum bulk stations; captive or private facilities; non-U.S. operations; shells; and businesses not realistically transferable as control investments in the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. LMM operators providing recurring outsourced nonrefrigerated specialized physical-goods storage and handling, including bulk petroleum, chemicals, automobiles, lumber, whiskey, and other goods requiring dedicated tanks, yards, buildings, security, equipment, or regulatory controls.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for all NAICS 493 warehousing rather than this six-digit specialized-goods lens, and the NIST evidence identifies use cases rather than wage-weighted task hours. I map inventory reconciliation, space and flow planning, scheduling, documentation, billing, compliance support, customer communication, exception triage, and some equipment-dispatch decisions into exposed work while excluding physical movement, maintenance, inspection, emergency response, and already automated controls. Broad warehousing is likely upward-biased because fulfillment-heavy operations contain more order-picking, clerical, and routing work than specialized tank, yard, and secure-storage facilities.
caveats: The occupation mix covers all warehousing and storage, not 493190 or the selected subsegment.
The fixed labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a cross-code IPS harmonization, so its downstream economic weight has a vintage and mapping mismatch.
The specialized-goods lens itself spans tank, yard, building, and high-security operating models with different task mixes.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit employer payroll extract by occupation and a time-and-motion study across petroleum, chemical, automobile, lumber, and beverage-storage operators, separating already automated tasks from remaining work, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: The broad warehousing workforce is predominantly physical: BLS reports 75.38% in transportation and material-moving occupations, including 14.97% industrial-truck operators, 23.31% manual freight movers, and 23.62% stockers/order fillers, while material-recording and scheduling occupations are 5.38%. AI can support the planning, records, allocation, dispatch, and exception layer documented by NIST, but much of specialized-storage labor remains embodied, safety-critical, or site-specific.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for all NAICS 493 warehousing and storage rising from 134.7 in 2024 to 169.7 in 2034, about 2.3% annually and approximately 12% over five years. I haircut the five-year midpoint to 6% because broad sector growth includes general merchandise and e-commerce fulfillment excluded from the lens, then widen for industrial production, commodity and automobile cycles, oil-transition exposure, customer insourcing, and product-specific storage duration. The broad projection is probably upward-biased for mature specialized storage but could understate niche reshoring or compliance-driven outsourcing.
caveats: BLS output projections cover all warehousing and storage and are not quantity forecasts for the selected lens.
Nominal revenue, real output, capacity, throughput, and storage-days can diverge materially.
The petroleum component faces transition and regulatory risks that may differ sharply by product and geography.
d5.change_evidence: Subsegment forecasts for capacity, occupied storage-days, throughput, utilization, outsourcing share, and contract pricing across petroleum, chemicals, automobiles, lumber, and beverage storage through 2031 would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Specialized goods still require physical custody, security, staging, and handling, and outsourcing avoids customers' dedicated capital investment. Demand should therefore remain broadly durable, but the selected lens lacks the strong fulfillment tailwind of general warehousing and is exposed to cyclical industrial volumes, petroleum transition, site closures, and customer concentration.

caveats: No public source provides the selected subsegment's share of current LMM firms.
The 246-firm frozen estimate uses a transportation-sector EBITDA-to-sales margin bridge that may misclassify asset-heavy specialty-storage operators.
NAICS assignment may follow an establishment's primary activity even when the parent combines storage, transport, wholesaling, or real estate.
e.change_evidence: A Business Register or commercial-database census linking each 493190 firm to questionnaire product line, ultimate owner, captive status, establishment count, revenue model, normalized EBITDA, and transaction history would measure eligibility directly.
e.rationale: The frozen n=246 covers the full 493190 code, whereas the coherent lens excludes household-goods and paper-records operators explicitly enumerated by the Census questionnaire. Within specialized storage, captive industrial terminals, petroleum merchants, large strategic networks, real-estate-like assets, and firms outside the EBITDA band further reduce eligibility. I therefore judge that roughly one-quarter to somewhat over half of the frozen full-code candidate count could match the independent, transferable service-firm lens, pending firm-level verification.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 493190 ($3,689,251,831) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 493190 ($8,323,758,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0983 (margins.json[49]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Transportation EBITDA/Sales 9.83% (couriers/warehousing folded into transport aggregate)); revenue/employee $135,324 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 493190); class <5: 528 firms, avg revenue/firm $653,534 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $64,242 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 157 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,603,879 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $157,661 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 102 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,291,961 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $323,600 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 207 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,554,763 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $546,033 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 159 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,079,107 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $695,876 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 246 firms, avg revenue/firm $20,795,484 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,044,196 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 246

o.bridge: Census establishes that warehousing operators take responsibility for stored goods, OSHA imposes site, equipment, training, and hazard-control duties, and EPA imposes prevention duties on qualifying oil-storage facilities. These sources demonstrate accountable physical operation but do not measure the exact share of five-year service quantity that cannot become software-only. I translate physical custody and legal accountability into a very high operator-required share, allowing limited customer insourcing, unattended or remotely supervised facilities, and direct-flow substitution. Regulation may overstate required on-site labor but not the need for an accountable operating entity.
caveats: Operator-required service does not imply full-time labor at every site; one team may supervise multiple facilities remotely.
EPA SPCC applicability depends on capacity, location, and discharge risk and does not cover every facility in the lens.
Customer insourcing can remove outsourced quantity without eliminating the underlying physical-storage task.
o.change_evidence: Regulator and insurer acceptance of unattended specialty-storage facilities, autonomous-site performance and incident data, and measured customer shifts to captive storage or direct shipment would materially move the estimate.
o.rationale: AI can reduce administrative and monitoring labor, but specialized goods still occupy tanks, yards, racks, or secure buildings and require receipt, preservation, inspection, movement, spill or hazard response, maintenance, and delivery. OSHA and EPA obligations attach to employers or owner-operators, making pure software substitution implausible within five years even where remote supervision grows.

q.bridge: A current public-warehousing operator describes storage charges per pallet, square foot, or cubic foot plus handling, transaction, value-added, technology, and account fees, with activity-based, hybrid, all-inclusive, and cost-plus structures. This is provider-authored broad warehousing evidence, not a representative contract sample for specialized 493190 firms. I infer that unit and capacity pricing permits initial retention of labor and error savings, then reduce retention for cost-plus contracts, renewal and rebid pressure, volume tiers, shared productivity commitments, and customers' insourcing options. Broad public-warehouse flexibility may overstate repricing pressure relative to dedicated specialty contracts.
caveats: The source is a service provider's guide and may present favorable marketing claims.
Chemical, petroleum, automobile, lumber, and beverage-storage contracts may price and allocate savings differently.
The estimate concerns retained unit-economics benefit, not demand growth or throughput gains.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract and invoice panel showing pricing basis, renewal cadence, indexation, cost-plus or gain-sharing clauses, automation savings, churn, and post-deployment price changes would directly estimate capture.
q.rationale: Most listed prices attach to space, time, transactions, or output rather than labor hours, so lower internal cost need not trigger an immediate customer rebate. Over five years, sophisticated industrial buyers can demand rate relief at renewal, invoke gain-sharing or cost-plus terms, consolidate volume, or develop captive capacity; service reliability and compliance improvements can support some continued capture.

rho.bridge: BLS documents increasing sector-wide adoption of WMS, AGVs, robots, and AI, while NIST documents concrete warehouse AI opportunities but not five-year implementation at LMM 493190 firms. I translate demonstrated sector deployment and use cases into an implementation share, then discount for heterogeneous legacy assets, systems integration, limited data, capital constraints, cybersecurity, safety validation, regulatory obligations, and low transaction density at some specialized sites. Evidence from larger and more standardized warehouses likely overstates implementation for smaller specialty operators.
caveats: The evidence establishes capability and direction, not a representative adoption rate.
Some productivity gains attributed to AI are inseparable from conventional WMS, sensors, robotics, and process redesign.
Hazardous-material and oil-storage obligations can delay or constrain autonomous operation.
rho.change_evidence: A representative survey of LMM specialty warehouses showing installed systems, deployment cost, integration time, uptime, insurer or regulator acceptance, and measured headcount or hour changes would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: AI layered onto WMS, sensors, digital twins, and dispatch systems can improve space allocation, incoming-flow planning, workforce planning, bottleneck detection, inventory accuracy, and equipment routing. More than a token share of the exposed task base should therefore be implementable within five years, but forklifts, racks, tanks, hazardous materials, and mixed legacy controls require disciplined integration and retain human accountability.

s5.bridge: FST's 2024 acquisition of Dancer Logistics is an observed control transfer involving warehousing and transportation, but it is broad adjacent 3PL evidence rather than a transaction in the narrowly selected specialty-storage lens. I use it only to establish that private warehousing operators transact and bound the five-year probability conservatively because no eligible-firm denominator, seller-age distribution, or complete private-deal census is available. Publicized acquisitions likely overstate ordinary transfer frequency.
caveats: One adjacent transaction cannot establish an industry transfer rate.
The cited target included transportation and temperature-controlled activities outside the selected lens.
Private asset sales and undisclosed family transfers may be absent from public sources.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year proprietary transaction census matched to all eligible specialty-storage operators, plus owner age, succession plan, asset ownership, environmental liabilities, and sale-process outcomes, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Asset-backed regional warehousing businesses can be integrated by larger operators for footprint, customers, staff, and operating efficiencies. However, specialized facilities have limited buyer pools, environmental and safety diligence, customer concentration, and site-specific permits, while many operators may transfer internally, sell assets without an operating company, or remain captive.

principal_driver: The frozen 246-firm full-code estimate and recurring outsourced custody economics create a potentially meaningful acquisition universe, while AI can improve the information and coordination layer without threatening the underlying physical service.
principal_weakness: Severe code heterogeneity and the absence of subsegment-specific firm, occupation, adoption, transaction, and demand data make the apparent universe and all operating primitives highly proxy-dependent.

sources.supports: Defines warehousing as custody of goods for others with responsibility for security and lists 493190 examples including bulk petroleum, lumber, document, and whiskey storage.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definitions: Warehousing and Storage and 493190 Other Warehousing and Storage

sources.supports: Separately enumerates 493190 household-goods storage, paper-document storage, and specialized-goods storage including petroleum, chemicals, automobile dead storage, and other special storage; also enumerates services and technology use.
sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Warehousing and Storage Questionnaire

sources.supports: Provides broad warehousing occupation shares, including 75.38% transportation and material moving, 14.97% industrial truck operators, 23.31% manual freight movers, 23.62% stockers and order fillers, and 5.38% material-recording and scheduling occupations.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 493000

sources.supports: States that warehousing firms increasingly use warehouse-management systems, automated vehicles, robots, and AI and that productivity gains should restrain labor demand.
sources.title: Industry and Occupational Employment Projections Overview and Highlights, 2024-34

sources.supports: Documents warehouse AI opportunities in dynamic location allocation, forklift and transport dispatch, digital twins, bottleneck detection, workforce planning, and incoming-material flow.
sources.title: Artificial Intelligence in the Supply Chain: Workshop Report

sources.supports: Documents warehouse duties and hazards involving forklifts, loading, racks, chemicals, hazardous materials, training, and safe integration of robotics and automation.
sources.title: Warehousing: Hazards and Solutions

sources.supports: Explains SPCC applicability to qualifying nontransportation facilities that store or transfer oil, demonstrating operator-level prevention obligations for the petroleum portion of the lens.
sources.title: Does the Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure Rule Apply to Your Facility?

sources.supports: Projects broad NAICS 493 warehousing and storage real output from 134.7 in 2024 to 169.7 in 2034, an annual growth rate of 2.3%.
sources.title: Industry Employment and Output Projections, 2024-34

sources.supports: Documents a July 2024 acquisition of a regional warehousing and transportation company, providing adjacent evidence that private warehouse operators transfer control.
sources.title: FST Logistics Acquires Dancer Logistics, Expanding Its Footprint in the Midwest

sources.supports: Describes storage, handling, transaction, value-added, technology, and account fees; activity-based, hybrid, all-inclusive, cost-plus, tiered, and committed-capacity pricing; specialized handling; and public-warehouse demand drivers.
sources.title: Complete Guide to Public Warehousing Services

title: Other Warehousing and Storage
